author: Ruohang Feng (rh@vonng.com)
description: One Kafka broker/controller selected by ins: role and liveness, replication and KRaft state, request path saturation, JVM and process health, plus host resources joined through ip. Explains why this instance is slow, busy, unstable or down.

panels.description: Identity strips of this Kafka process, taken from its JMX scrape target labels (`up{role=~".+"}`) plus the live KRaft state (`kafka_server_raft_state == 1`): instance name, cluster (links to Kafka Overview), node IP (links to Node Instance), KRaft node id, static role (combined teal / controller blue / broker green) and current Raft state (leader blue / follower green).
Partitions hosted and led by this broker, plus reassignment churn and offline replicas. Under-replication detail lives in the Replication section.
panels.title: Partition Ownership

panels.description: JMX endpoint and Kafka JVM availability. The legend carries process role and KRaft node id.
JVM uptime: `time() - process_start_time_seconds` of the Kafka process. Amber below 5 minutes to make a fresh (possibly unexplained) restart obvious; teal afterwards.
JVM heap utilization: `kafka:ins:jvm_heap_used_ratio` = heap used / heap max. Amber ≥75%, red ≥90% — sustained high heap turns into GC pressure and request latency before it turns into OOM.
Kafka JVM CPU consumption in cores (`kafka:ins:jvm_cpu_cores` = rate of process CPU seconds). Amber ≥2, red ≥4 cores. Compare with host CPU in the Node section to separate Kafka load from neighbours.
Open file descriptors as a share of the limit (`process_open_fds / process_max_fds`). Kafka holds an fd per log segment and per connection; amber ≥70%, red ≥90% — exhaustion is fatal for the broker.
JMX exporter collection time per scrape (`jmx_scrape_duration_seconds`). The MBean allow-list keeps this bounded; amber ≥3s, red ≥8s (scrape timeout is 10s — beyond it the target flaps DOWN).

Navigation: topics hosted by the cluster this instance belongs to, with partition count (`kafka_topic_partitions`) and 5-minute append rate (`kafka:topic:msg_rate5m`). Topic and partition state is cluster-scoped (collected by kafka_exporter, not by this broker); click a topic to open the Kafka Topic dashboard.
panels.title: Topics

panels.description: Navigation: consumer groups on this cluster with member count (`kafka_consumergroup_members`), total backlog (`kafka:csg_topic:lag` summed per group) and 5-minute commit rate. Group state is cluster-scoped; click a group to open the Kafka Consumer dashboard.
panels.title: Consumer Groups

panels.description: Records appended per second to partitions this broker LEADS (`rate(kafka_server_broker_messages_in_total)`). Follower and consumer traffic do not count here — a broker with zero messages-in can still serve fetches and replicate.
panels.title: Messages In

panels.description: Alert timeline in the standard Pigsty style: solid bands are firing alerts (`ALERTS{alertstate="firing"}`), pale bands are pending ones still inside their for-window, for every alert carrying this cluster label. Click a legend entry to inspect it in Alertmanager.

Replication safety on this broker: partitions below full ISR (`under_replicated_partitions`), partitions below min.insync.replicas (`under_min_isr_partitions` — producers with acks=all fail on these) and offline log directories. All three belong at zero.
panels.title: Replication Health

panels.description: Bytes flowing through this broker, split by purpose: client traffic (`kafka_server_broker_bytes_in/out_total`) vs replication traffic (`kafka_server_broker_replication_bytes_in/out_total`). Replication-in ≈ what this broker follows, replication-out ≈ what it feeds to followers as a leader. A follower catching up shows replication-in far above client-in.
panels.title: Network Throughput

panels.description: ISR shrink and expand operations per second (`kafka_server_replica_manager_isr_shrinks/expands_total`) plus failed ISR updates. Churn means followers keep falling behind and re-catching-up — look at disk I/O, network, or GC pauses on the followers.
panels.title: ISR Churn

panels.description: Client-visible request failures on this broker: failed produce / fetch request rates (`kafka_server_broker_failed_produce/fetch_requests_total`) plus the per-API error-code breakdown (`kafka_network_request_errors_total{error!="NONE"}`). The error code names the cause directly — e.g. NOT_ENOUGH_REPLICAS (under-min-ISR), REQUEST_TIMED_OUT, UNKNOWN_TOPIC_OR_PARTITION.
panels.title: Request Errors

panels.description: Current KRaft quorum state. Controllers normally report leader/follower; brokers normally report observer.
panels.title: KRaft Member State

panels.description: KRaft metadata log positions on this node: log end offset vs high watermark (`kafka_server_raft_log_end_offset/high_watermark`), with the quorum epoch on the right axis. LEO−HW divergence means slow commit on this node; epoch increments are controller elections.
panels.title: Metadata Log Progress

panels.description: Controller view of the cluster: active broker count vs fenced brokers (`kafka_controller_active/fenced_broker_count`). A fenced broker missed its heartbeats and had its leaders migrated away — it serves nothing until it re-registers.
panels.title: Controller Health

panels.description: How far this node lags applying the cluster metadata log: broker/controller last-applied-record lag in seconds, plus metadata snapshot age. Sustained apply lag means this node acts on a stale view of the cluster.
panels.title: Metadata Propagation

panels.description: Controller event queue wait time (`kafka_controller_event_queue_time_seconds` p50/p95). Meaningful on the active controller only; rising queue time delays every metadata change (topic creation, leader election, reassignment) cluster-wide.
panels.title: Controller Event Latency

panels.description: API request throughput by request type (`rate(kafka_network_request_total)` summed by request). The mix characterizes the workload: Produce/Fetch dominate data flow; Metadata/Heartbeat/ApiVersions dominate idle or chatty-client clusters.
panels.title: Request Rate

panels.description: Total request time p95/p99 in seconds by API (`kafka_network_request_total_time_seconds` summary quantiles), restricted to data-path and group-coordination APIs so the panel stays readable. Total = queue + local + remote + response send time.
panels.title: Request Latency

panels.description: Request-path saturation: request/response queue depth, purgatory sizes per delayed operation (parked long-poll Fetches are NORMAL and large), and idle ratios of request handlers / network processors on the right axis. Idle sustained below 10% fires the saturation alerts — the broker is thread-starved.
panels.title: Queues & Saturation

panels.description: JVM memory pools: heap used/committed and non-heap used (`jvm_memory_used/committed_bytes`). The heap sawtooth is normal young-gen cycling — watch the sawtooth FLOOR: a rising floor is live-set growth from workload or leak.
panels.title: JVM Memory

panels.description: GC cost per collector: `rate(jvm_gc_collection_seconds_sum)` (seconds of GC per second, i.e. share of wall time) and collection frequency. Sustained >0.05 s/s means 5% of time in GC — expect request latency jitter.
panels.title: Garbage Collection

panels.description: Thread census by state (`jvm_threads_state`) and deadlocked-thread count (`jvm_threads_deadlocked` — any nonzero fires KafkaJvmDeadlock CRIT). Growth in BLOCKED threads points at lock contention in the request path.
panels.title: JVM Threads

panels.description: NIO buffer usage (`jvm_buffer_pool_used_bytes`): direct buffers back network I/O, mapped buffers back log segment files. Mapped size tracking on-disk log size is expected, not a leak.
panels.title: JVM Buffer Pools

panels.description: Kafka JVM CPU (cores) overlaid with whole-host CPU utilization (right axis, node_exporter via ip join). Separates "Kafka is busy" from "the host is busy with something else" (compaction, backups, noisy neighbours).
panels.title: Process & Host Pressure

panels.description: Host CPU and memory utilization from node_exporter, joined by this instance's ip. Note Kafka deliberately runs with high memory usage — the page cache IS the read path; judge memory by pressure and swap, not by percent used.
panels.title: Node CPU / Memory

panels.description: Host NIC receive/transmit throughput (`node_network_*_bytes`). The NIC ceiling caps broker throughput; compare with the broker's own bytes in/out to attribute traffic to Kafka vs other processes.
panels.title: Node Network

panels.description: Disk bandwidth and IOPS on this host (`node_disk_*`). Kafka writes sequentially and normally reads from page cache — heavy sustained READS mean consumers fell out of cache and are streaming cold segments from disk.
panels.title: Node Disk I/O

panels.description: Device utilization and IO latency (`node_disk_io_time`, await). Utilization pinned near 100% with rising await marks the log device as the bottleneck — request latency and ISR churn follow.
panels.title: Disk Pressure

panels.description: Filesystem fill level on this host (`node_filesystem_*`). A full log directory is marked offline by Kafka (KafkaOfflineLogDirectory CRIT) and drops every replica it holds — keep headroom above retention peaks.
panels.title: Filesystem Usage

panels.description: JMX exporter self-health on this target: scrape duration, error flag (`jmx_scrape_error` — reachable process but failed MBean collection), and cached bean count. Distinguishes exporter trouble from Kafka trouble.
panels.title: JMX Exporter

panels.description: Raw Kafka server logs from VictoriaLogs: LogsQL `job:syslog (unit:kafka OR app:kafka)` filtered to the member IPs of this scope (log streams carry node identity, so filtering is by ip, not by kafka cls). Fields: level, ip, unit, message.

targets.expr: ip:$ip job:syslog (unit:kafka OR app:kafka) | fields level,unit,_msg

panels.title: Kafka Service Logs

title: Kafka Instance
